input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, with emphasis on its first axis consisting of a motor and gearbox that is controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. The robot operates in a manufacturing environment, requires high‑precision motion, and its mechanical and control components are the focus of the digital twin implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides a real‑time visualization dashboard for the robot’s first‑axis data, performs diagnostic analysis to identify joint friction, and delivers motion‑accuracy optimization by predicting and suggesting corrective actions. These services cover monitoring, fault detection, and performance optimisation for operators and maintenance engineers.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation is based on Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using systems‑engineering modeling languages such as SysML. Architectural description languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML internal block diagrams are used to define components, interfaces and their composition. Behavioural models can be expressed with state machines in SysML v1.7 or with MontiArc Automaton. Simulation and data‑calculation models are executed by tools such as MathWorks Simulink and other simulation engines. Generated code may be written in general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. A processing component consumes input data traces, applies the associated simulation or calculation models, and produces output data that become part of the digital shadow, with full traceability as described in the reference model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin described relies on a heterogeneous set of computational models and data artifacts anchored in Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE). Structural and behavioral aspects are captured with SysML diagrams (including internal block diagrams and state‑machine models) and with Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, which define components, interfaces, and their interconnections. Execution‑oriented behavior is realized through simulation models built in MathWorks Simulink, while additional code may be expressed in general‑purpose languages like C++ or Java. The data side consists of contextual data traces that are aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes, forming the digital‑shadow layer that feeds the twin. Model relationships are managed by linking high‑level SysML specifications to lower‑level simulation models, enabling traceability from requirements through to executable simulations. Both physics‑based (white‑box) models and data‑driven (black‑box) models are combined, allowing the twin to exploit analytical insight and empirical patterns within the same constellation.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by integrating both white‑box Simulink simulations (providing clear interpretability and predictability) and black‑box learning models (capturing complex non‑linear relationships). Validation and verification focus on ensuring the correctness of model transformations, such as from SysML specifications to simulation models, with traceability mechanisms highlighted to track changes. Reliability is linked to the correctness of underlying models and data transformations, prompting systematic verification of transformation steps and verification of model outputs against physical measurements. While explicit uncertainty quantification methods are not detailed, the emphasis on traceability and verification implies a quality‑assurance process aimed at detecting and correcting model errors.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital shadow is defined as a passive entity with an automated one‑way data flow from the physical object to the digital object; it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system. Therefore, no command types, control message formats, validation, authentication, feedback, or execution confirmation mechanisms are present.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for creating a Digital Twin is driven by a reference‑model approach. A Digital Shadow reference model (expressed as a UML class diagram) is first instantiated through a concretization step that maps concrete classes and associations to the abstract concepts, establishing a formal conformance relation. Two design pathways are supported: (1) a data‑driven path that starts from the data traces available from the cyber‑physical system, and (2) a purpose‑driven path that begins with the intended use‑case and required insights. The method then proceeds with (i) gathering all relevant data traces from the CPS, (ii) defining processing components that consume these input traces and invoke engineering, simulation, or data‑calculation models to generate output traces, (iii) encoding the processing logic in simulation or calculation models, and (iv) linking each output trace to its origin via the “originates‑from” association, thereby preserving transitive provenance and fine‑grained traceability. The overall workflow leverages Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for requirements, functional, and logical architecture, and architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for component‑connector specifications. Evolution is managed by updating the concrete realization and the associated processing components while maintaining alignment with the reference model and its conformance constraints.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin implementation adheres to multiple established modeling standards. System structure and behavior are captured using Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) with SysML (Systems Modeling Language) for requirements, functional, and logical architectures. Component‑connector specifications rely on architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, as well as SysML internal block diagrams (IBDs). The Digital Shadow reference model is expressed as a UML (Unified Modeling Language) class diagram, providing a formal metamodel that can be concretized through a defined conformance relation. These standards ensure interoperability with simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) and code generators for languages like C++ and Java.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[62] Z. Huang, Y. Shen, J. Li, M. Fey, and C. Brecher, “A survey on aidriven digital twins in industry 4.0: Smart manufacturing and advanced
robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)
[12-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

[66] H. M. Muctadir, D. A. Manrique Negrin, R. Gunasekaran, L. Cleophas,
M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the MABI MAX 100 robot, its specific components (first axis motor and gearbox), the controller type (Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl), and the operational context (manufacturing, high precision). This is fully supported by the provided text. Quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'first axis... consisting of the motor and gearbox', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction correctly captures the visualization dashboard and the diagnostic analysis of joint friction for accuracy improvement. However, it includes details not explicitly present in the text, such as the specific user roles ('operators and maintenance engineers') and the service 'predicting and suggesting corrective actions' (the text states data is 'applied to improve the robot control’s accuracy' but does not detail predictive suggestions). Quotes: 'visualization in a dashboard', 'derive the friction... within the gearbox', 'improve robot motion accuracy'.

The extraction provides a highly accurate and detailed summary of the tools and enablers described in Section II.A of the source. It correctly identifies 'Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)', 'SysML', 'MontiArc', and 'MathWorks Simulink', and accurately describes their roles in modeling, simulation, and processing data traces.

modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies

systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,

Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document does not provide specific details on synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity. While it mentions 'live measurements' and 'real-time identification' in the abstract and introduction, these are general descriptors rather than technical specifications of time scales. Therefore, the statement 'Not in Document' is accurate regarding detailed temporal characteristics.

The text describes a specific implementation of a digital shadow for 'the first axis of the robot'. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures, or centralized/decentralized architectures. The focus is on a single shadow instance. Therefore, the statement 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The paper describes the construction of a specific digital shadow model using a reference model (DSRM) and concrete components like AAS and SysML. However, it does not describe a broader 'Digital Twin constellation' involving orchestration across multiple distinct twin systems or enterprise-wide resource management. The scope is limited to the internal architecture of the shadow itself.

The text mentions that sensor data is 'streamed via MQTT' (Section IV.A.4), which is a data exchange protocol. However, it does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, broader interoperability standards beyond this specific protocol, or integration with other Digital Twins. The description 'Not in Document' is mostly accurate, though there is a minor mention of a protocol.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is comprehensive and strongly supported by the source text. It accurately lists the MBSE approach, specific modeling languages (SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc), and execution tools (Simulink, C++, Java). It correctly defines the data artifacts as contextual data traces and captures the combination of white-box and black-box models. Quotes: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc', 'MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java', 'set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction', 'analytical (white-box) and data-driven (black-box) friction models'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the physical actuators and control mechanisms of the robot's first axis. Supported by quotes: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'followed by a cycloidal gear', and 'The axes can be controlled separately. Position, speed, or acceleration can serve as inputs'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text details the sensing infrastructure, specifically the encoders used for position feedback. Supported by quotes: 'There are two position encoders per drive', 'The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox', and 'We have access to time series of all sensors'.

The extraction accurately reflects the paper's approach to fidelity by combining white-box and black-box models and correctly identifies the primary validation method: comparing simulated data against measured data. It also correctly highlights traceability as a mechanism for reliability and quality assurance. Some details, such as 'clear interpretability and predictability' for white-box models and 'verification of model transformations,' are interpretive rather than explicit text, but the core technical content is well-supported. Quotes: 'deviation of simulated data versus measured data', 'data that remains fully traceable to its origin and method of computation', 'combining... white-box and black-box friction models'.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The document explicitly describes the digital shadow as having 'an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object' and mentions 'live measurements from the robot' and 'sensor-based Measurements'. While it does not list every specific protocol or frequency detail, the core concept of physical-to-virtual interaction is present. Therefore, the extraction stating 'Not in Document' is incorrect.

Accurately describes the digital shadow as a passive entity with no return data flow or control commands. This is strongly supported by the source text: 'a digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.'

The document mentions that 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'. This provides a specific technical detail regarding the communication protocol. The extraction stating 'Not in Document' ignores this evidence.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents mention 'visualization in a dashboard' and 'executable digital shadow', but they do not provide information regarding hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational requirements, or specific deployment models. Therefore, the assessment that the information is missing is correct.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions the 'development phase' and 'operation', but it does not provide a description of the Digital Twin's lifecycle phases, representation types at different phases, transition management, or lifecycle governance. The content focuses on the creation process rather than the lifecycle stages.

The extraction is highly accurate and detailed. It captures the reference-model approach, the concretization step, and the two design pathways (data-driven and purpose-driven) described in the text. It correctly lists the steps for creating the digital shadow: gathering data traces, defining processing components, encoding logic in models, and linking outputs via 'originates-from' for traceability. The mention of evolution by updating the concrete realization is also supported. Quotes: 'In a concretization development step, one can come up with such a conforming model', 'The first option is to come up from the data... The second option is purpose-driven', and 'By following the originates from association, one can trace a data trace back to its generating processing component'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the source text mentions that the digital shadow enables 'better understanding of the physical relationships' and is used to 'improve robot control’s accuracy' by detecting friction, it does not provide details on general decision-making processes, analytics capabilities, user interaction methods (other than a dashboard), or confidence measures for recommendations. The text focuses on the engineering of the shadow rather than its decision-support features. Thus, the claim that this is not in the document is correct.

The extraction is correct and specific to the source text. It accurately identifies the standards mentioned: MBSE, SysML, UML, and ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc). It correctly describes their usage (requirements, functional/logical architecture, component-connector specifications) and the interoperability with tools like Simulink and languages like C++ and Java. Quotes: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'architecture description languages (ADLs) are used... e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc', and 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram'.
